title: Scrotal Redness and Swelling in Cats: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Identify causes of scrotal swelling in cats. Learn why this condition requires prompt care and how photos help your vet triage the issue.
content_html:
What is Scrotal Redness and Swelling?

Redness and swelling of the scrotum in cats, also known as scrotal dermatitis or orchitis, is an inflammatory response that can be quite painful for your pet. This condition involves the skin surrounding the testicles or the testicles themselves becoming inflamed, often appearing bright red, warm to the touch, and significantly larger than normal. Common causes include local trauma, bacterial infections, insect bites, or contact allergies to bedding and cleaning supplies.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is categorized as Medium. While scrotal swelling is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it is a source of intense discomfort and potential systemic infection. You should seek veterinary care within 24 hours, especially if your cat is lethargic, has a reduced appetite, or is excessively licking and biting at the area, as this can lead to secondary infections or self-mutilation.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear, well-lit photo of the affected area can be incredibly beneficial for a veterinary triage team. Visual evidence allows a professional to differentiate between a localized skin irritation and a more serious condition, such as a scrotal abscess or a hernia. By seeing the severity of the inflammation, your veterinarian can better advise you on how to manage the pain at home until your appointment and prioritize your pet's needs accordingly.

faqs.question: Can I use over-the-counter creams on my cat?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid applying any human creams or ointments without veterinary approval, as cats often lick these areas and many human medications are toxic if ingested.

faqs.question: What can I do to keep my cat comfortable?
faqs.answer: The most important step is to prevent your cat from licking the area. Using an Elizabethan collar (a cone) will stop further irritation and self-trauma until you can see a vet.

faqs.question: What are the common symptoms to watch for?
faqs.answer: Look for visible redness, heat in the area, reluctance to move, a stiff-legged gait, or vocalizing when the hindquarters are touched.
